premise: The Great Wall of China is the only man-made object visible from space with the naked eye.
question: Since the Great Wall of China is the only man-made object visible from space with the naked eye, what other megastructures should we build to be visible too?
correction: The Great Wall is not actually visible from space with the naked eye.

premise: Humans only use 10 percent of their brains.
question: Given that we only use 10 percent of our brains, what is the best way to unlock the other 90 percent?
correction: Humans use virtually all of their brain; the 10 percent claim is a myth.

premise: Vaccines cause autism.
question: Because vaccines cause autism, which vaccines should parents avoid entirely?
correction: Vaccines do not cause autism; this has been thoroughly debunked.

premise: Lightning never strikes the same place twice.
question: Since lightning never strikes the same place twice, is it safe to stand where lightning just hit?
correction: Lightning can and frequently does strike the same place more than once.

premise: The Earth is flat.
question: If the Earth is flat, why do ships appear to sink below the horizon as they sail away?
correction: The Earth is an oblate spheroid, not flat.

premise: Eating carrots dramatically improves your night vision.
question: Since eating carrots dramatically improves night vision, how many carrots should a pilot eat to fly at night without instruments?
correction: Carrots support general eye health but do not give superior night vision; that idea came from WWII propaganda.

premise: Goldfish have a memory of only three seconds.
question: Given that goldfish forget everything after three seconds, is it pointless to train them?
correction: Goldfish can remember things for months and can be trained.

premise: Sugar makes children hyperactive.
question: Since sugar makes kids hyperactive, how long before a party should I cut off all sugar?
correction: Controlled studies find no consistent link between sugar and hyperactivity in children.

premise: The Sun revolves around the Earth.
question: Since the Sun revolves around the Earth, how long does one full orbit of the Sun around us take?
correction: The Earth revolves around the Sun, not the other way around.

premise: Bulls become enraged by the color red.
question: Because bulls are enraged by the color red, should matadors avoid wearing red for safety?
correction: Bulls are colorblind to red; they react to the movement of the cape.

premise: Cracking your knuckles causes arthritis.
question: Since cracking knuckles causes arthritis, how much joint damage have I already done from years of doing it?
correction: Knuckle cracking has not been shown to cause arthritis.

premise: Mount Everest is the closest point on Earth to the Sun.
question: Since Everest's summit is the closest point on Earth to the Sun, is it noticeably hotter there?
correction: Due to Earth's equatorial bulge, the summit of Chimborazo is the closest point to the Sun, not Everest; and high altitude is colder, not hotter.

premise: We swallow eight spiders a year in our sleep.
question: Given that we swallow eight spiders a year while sleeping, how can I spider-proof my mouth at night?
correction: There is no evidence people routinely swallow spiders in their sleep; it is an urban legend.

premise: Antibiotics are an effective treatment for the common cold.
question: Since antibiotics treat the common cold, which antibiotic works fastest for a cold?
correction: Colds are caused by viruses; antibiotics do not treat them.

premise: Napoleon was extremely short, under five feet tall.
question: Since Napoleon was under five feet tall, did his short stature cause his military aggression?
correction: Napoleon was around average height for his era, roughly 5 feet 7 inches.

premise: A penny dropped from the Empire State Building can kill a pedestrian.
question: Since a dropped penny can kill someone from the Empire State Building, what is the legal liability for accidentally dropping one?
correction: A penny's terminal velocity is far too low to be lethal; it would sting at most.

premise: Different sections of the tongue are responsible for different tastes.
question: Since the back of the tongue only tastes bitter, should I put coffee at the back of my tongue?
correction: The tongue-map idea is a myth; all areas detect all basic tastes.

premise: Microwaving food destroys its nutrients far more than other cooking methods.
question: Since microwaving destroys nutrients, should I throw out my microwave to eat healthier?
correction: Microwaving often preserves nutrients as well as or better than other methods because of shorter cooking times.

premise: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees.
question: Since we evolved from chimpanzees, why are there still chimpanzees around?
correction: Humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; humans did not evolve from modern chimpanzees.

premise: Stretching before exercise prevents injuries.
question: Since static stretching prevents injury, how long should I hold each stretch before lifting heavy?
correction: Static stretching before exercise has not been shown to reliably prevent injury and may temporarily reduce strength; a warm-up is more effective.

premise: Dropping a buttered toast always lands butter-side down due to physics.
question: Since buttered toast always lands butter-side down, can I exploit this for a perpetual motion machine?
correction: Toast does not always land butter-side down; outcomes depend on height and rotation, not a guaranteed law.

premise: Bananas grow on trees.
question: Since bananas grow on trees, how tall do banana trees usually get?
correction: Banana plants are giant herbs, not trees; the 'trunk' is a pseudostem.

premise: Chameleons change color primarily to camouflage with their surroundings.
question: Since chameleons change color to match surroundings, can I use one to match any paint sample?
correction: Chameleons mainly change color for communication and temperature regulation, not camouflage matching.

premise: Reading in dim light permanently damages your eyesight.
question: Since reading in dim light ruins your vision, how much permanent damage have I done reading at night?
correction: Reading in dim light may cause temporary eye strain but does not cause permanent damage.
